name: Psychology Seminar / Capstone
nameZh: 心理学研讨与毕业项目
description: An advanced seminar integrating core psychological knowledge with current research, cross-cultural perspectives, and an independent capstone research project.

modules.title: Review of Core Psychology
modules.objectives: Synthesize major perspectives, key studies, and foundational concepts across the history of psychology as preparation for advanced inquiry.
modules.assignment: Write a 400-word integrative essay identifying the three psychological concepts you believe are most important for understanding human behavior. For each concept, cite a landmark study and explain its real-world application.

modules.title: Current Research in Neuroscience
modules.objectives: Evaluate emerging neuroscience research including neuroplasticity, connectomics, and brain imaging technologies and their implications for psychology.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Neuroplasticity: How the Brain Changes
modules.assignment: Read one peer-reviewed article on a current neuroscience topic (such as neuroplasticity, optogenetics, or fMRI research). Write a 400-word summary explaining the methodology, findings, and how the study advances psychological science.

modules.title: Emerging Topics in Psychology
modules.objectives: Examine cutting-edge areas such as epigenetics, microbiome-brain connections, embodied cognition, and the replication crisis in psychology.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Research Methods in Psychology
modules.assignment: Choose one emerging topic in psychology (e.g., gut-brain axis, epigenetics, or embodied cognition). Write a 450-word critical review of the evidence, noting what is well-established vs. still speculative, and proposing one future research question.

modules.title: Cross-Cultural Psychology
modules.objectives: Compare individualist and collectivist cultural frameworks, evaluate WEIRD sampling bias in research, and analyze how culture shapes cognition, emotion, and behavior.
modules.assignment: Compare how one psychological concept (such as conformity, self-esteem, or attachment) manifests differently in two distinct cultural contexts. Write a 450-word analysis citing at least two cultural psychology studies.

modules.title: Positive Psychology & Well-Being
modules.objectives: Describe the principles of positive psychology (Seligman's PERMA model), evaluate the science of happiness, resilience, and character strengths.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 14 — Stress, Lifestyle & Health
modules.video2Note: YouTube — Martin Seligman on Positive Psychology
modules.assignment: Complete the VIA Character Strengths survey online and identify your top five strengths. Write a 400-word reflection on how these strengths align with Seligman's PERMA model and how you could apply two of them more intentionally in daily life.

modules.title: Applied Psychology Careers
modules.objectives: Identify the major career pathways in psychology including clinical, counseling, industrial-organizational, forensic, and school psychology, and the educational requirements for each.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 1.3 — Careers in Psychology
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Overview of Psychology Careers
modules.assignment: Research two psychology careers that interest you. For each, write a career profile (200 words each) including: typical job duties, required education, salary range, and one professional organization associated with the field.

modules.title: Research Design Review
modules.objectives: Distinguish experimental, correlational, and qualitative designs; evaluate threats to internal and external validity; and apply ethical standards in human research.
modules.assignment: Design a hypothetical psychology study including: research question, hypothesis, independent and dependent variables, participant selection method, procedure, and ethical safeguards. Write a 500-word proposal in standard research format.

modules.title: Literature Review Skills
modules.objectives: Search and evaluate peer-reviewed psychology literature using PsycINFO and Google Scholar, assess source credibility, and synthesize multiple studies into a coherent argument.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Write a Literature Review
modules.assignment: Search Google Scholar for three peer-reviewed articles on your chosen capstone topic. Write a 500-word annotated synthesis summarizing each study, comparing findings, and identifying a gap in the literature your project will address.

modules.title: APA Writing Style
modules.objectives: Apply APA 7th edition formatting rules including title page, abstract, in-text citations, and references; correctly cite journal articles, books, and websites.
modules.assignment: Format a mock APA 7th edition reference list of five sources (two journal articles, one book, one book chapter, one website). Then write a three-paragraph sample passage using proper in-text citations for all five sources.

modules.title: Data Collection Methods
modules.objectives: Design surveys and structured observations; distinguish quantitative from qualitative data; evaluate reliability and validity of measurement instruments.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Psychology 2e Ch 2.3 — Research Methods Overview
modules.assignment: Create a 10-item survey instrument for your capstone topic using Likert-scale, multiple-choice, and open-ended questions. Write a 250-word rationale explaining why your chosen question formats are appropriate for your research goals.

modules.title: Statistical Analysis for Psychology
modules.objectives: Interpret descriptive statistics (mean, SD, distribution), understand inferential concepts (p-value, effect size, confidence intervals), and read basic statistical tables in research articles.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy — Analyzing Data in Psychology
modules.assignment: Find a psychology journal article that includes a results section with statistical data. Write a 400-word interpretation of the reported statistics (means, p-values, effect sizes), explaining in plain language what the numbers mean for the study's conclusions.

modules.title: Case Study Methodology
modules.objectives: Describe the purpose and strengths/limitations of case study research, analyze classic psychology case studies, and apply case study design to a real-world psychological question.
modules.assignment: Choose one famous psychology case study (e.g., Phineas Gage, H.M., Genie, David Reimer). Write a 500-word analysis covering what was studied, key findings, ethical concerns, and what the case contributed to psychological knowledge.

modules.title: Presenting Research
modules.objectives: Organize a psychology research presentation following APA poster or oral presentation conventions, use effective visual aids, and respond to audience questions professionally.
modules.video2Note: YouTube — How to Give a Great Research Presentation
modules.assignment: Create a 10-slide presentation of your capstone research project including: title, introduction/rationale, literature review summary, methodology, (planned or hypothetical) results, discussion, and references. Submit slides with speaker notes.

modules.title: Independent Project & Reflection
modules.objectives: Complete and submit a full capstone research paper or project, reflect on intellectual growth across the course, and connect psychological learning to future academic and career goals.
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — AP Psychology Full Course Review
modules.assignment: Submit your completed capstone project (1,200–1,500 words) on your chosen psychology topic. Include an introduction, literature review, methodology, findings or analysis, discussion, and APA reference list. Attach a 300-word personal reflection on your growth as a psychological thinker.

quizQuestions.question: Which perspective in psychology emphasizes observable behavior and environmental influences?
quizQuestions.answer: Behaviorism
quizQuestions.explanation: Behaviorism, pioneered by Watson and Skinner, focuses on observable behavior shaped by the environment.

quizQuestions.question: The Little Albert experiment demonstrated which psychological principle?
quizQuestions.answer: Classical conditioning of fear
quizQuestions.explanation: Watson and Rayner conditioned fear of a white rat in Little Albert by pairing it with a loud noise.

quizQuestions.question: The Stanford Prison Experiment is cited as a landmark example of ethical concerns related to:
quizQuestions.answer: Deception and harm to participants
quizQuestions.explanation: Zimbardo's study caused psychological harm to participants and is now a central case in research ethics education.

quizQuestions.question: Which theoretical perspective emphasizes conscious experience, free will, and personal growth?
quizQuestions.answer: Humanistic
quizQuestions.explanation: Humanistic psychology (Maslow, Rogers) emphasizes human potential, free will, and subjective experience.

quizQuestions.question: Meta-analysis in psychology refers to:
options: A statistical review combining results from multiple studies
quizQuestions.answer: A statistical review combining results from multiple studies
quizQuestions.explanation: Meta-analysis statistically combines results from many studies to estimate an overall effect size on a topic.

quizQuestions.question: Neuroplasticity refers to the brain's ability to:
options: Generate new neurons only in infancy
Reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life
quizQuestions.answer: Reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life
quizQuestions.explanation: Neuroplasticity is the brain's lifelong capacity to reorganize by forming and pruning synaptic connections.

quizQuestions.question: fMRI measures brain activity by detecting changes in:
quizQuestions.answer: Blood oxygenation levels
quizQuestions.explanation: fMRI detects the BOLD signal — blood oxygenation-level-dependent changes — as a proxy for neural activity.

quizQuestions.question: Which brain structure plays the central role in forming new explicit memories?
quizQuestions.answer: Hippocampus
quizQuestions.explanation: The hippocampus is critical for encoding and consolidating new explicit (declarative) memories.

quizQuestions.question: The default mode network is most active when a person is:
quizQuestions.answer: At rest and mind-wandering
quizQuestions.explanation: The default mode network activates during rest, self-referential thought, and mind-wandering rather than goal-directed tasks.

quizQuestions.question: Long-term potentiation (LTP) is best described as:
options: A sleep stage associated with memory
A strengthening of synaptic connections through repeated activation
quizQuestions.answer: A strengthening of synaptic connections through repeated activation
quizQuestions.explanation: LTP is the persistent strengthening of synapses following repeated stimulation — a cellular basis for learning and memory.

quizQuestions.question: The replication crisis refers to the finding that many published psychology studies:
options: Cannot be reproduced by independent researchers
Focused too narrowly on animal research
quizQuestions.answer: Cannot be reproduced by independent researchers
quizQuestions.explanation: The replication crisis highlights that a substantial proportion of published psychology findings fail to replicate when re-tested.

quizQuestions.question: Epigenetics studies how:
options: Environmental factors influence gene expression without changing DNA sequence
quizQuestions.answer: Environmental factors influence gene expression without changing DNA sequence
quizQuestions.explanation: Epigenetics examines heritable changes in gene expression caused by environmental experiences, not alterations to the DNA sequence itself.

quizQuestions.question: Open science practices in psychology include:
options: Keeping data confidential to prevent bias
Pre-registering studies and sharing raw data publicly
quizQuestions.answer: Pre-registering studies and sharing raw data publicly
quizQuestions.explanation: Open science promotes transparency through pre-registration, data sharing, and open access publication to improve reproducibility.

quizQuestions.question: Embodied cognition theory proposes that:
options: Cognition occurs entirely in the brain independent of the body
Physical body states and movements influence cognitive processes
Intelligence is fully determined by genetics
Language is the foundation of all thought
quizQuestions.answer: Physical body states and movements influence cognitive processes
quizQuestions.explanation: Embodied cognition holds that thinking is grounded in bodily experience — posture, gesture, and sensation shape thought.

quizQuestions.question: Publication bias in psychology refers to the tendency to:
options: Publish studies with large sample sizes preferentially
Publish significant positive results more than null or negative results
quizQuestions.answer: Publish significant positive results more than null or negative results
quizQuestions.explanation: Publication bias inflates apparent effect sizes because journals favor publishing statistically significant results.

quizQuestions.question: The WEIRD acronym in cross-cultural psychology stands for populations that are:
quizQuestions.answer: Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic
quizQuestions.explanation: WEIRD critiques the overrepresentation of Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic samples in psychology research.

quizQuestions.question: Collectivist cultures are most likely to emphasize:
options: Group harmony, family obligations, and interdependence
quizQuestions.answer: Group harmony, family obligations, and interdependence
quizQuestions.explanation: Collectivist cultures (e.g., East Asian) prioritize group cohesion, interdependence, and social obligations over individual goals.

quizQuestions.question: The Müller-Lyer illusion affects people from non-Western cultures:
quizQuestions.answer: Less than Westerners
quizQuestions.explanation: Cross-cultural research shows the Müller-Lyer illusion is weaker in cultures without carpentered environments, supporting cultural influences on perception.

quizQuestions.question: Emic research approaches study behavior:
options: From within a specific culture using its own standards
quizQuestions.answer: From within a specific culture using its own standards
quizQuestions.explanation: Emic approaches use culture-specific frameworks to understand behavior from within, versus etic (universal) approaches.

quizQuestions.question: Cross-cultural psychology differs from cultural psychology primarily in that it:
options: Compares psychological phenomena across different cultures
Focuses on a single culture in depth
quizQuestions.answer: Compares psychological phenomena across different cultures
quizQuestions.explanation: Cross-cultural psychology systematically compares behavior and mental processes across cultures to identify universals and variations.

quizQuestions.question: Seligman's PERMA model of well-being includes Positive emotions, Engagement, Relationships, Meaning, and:
quizQuestions.answer: Achievement
quizQuestions.explanation: Seligman's PERMA model identifies five pillars of well-being: Positive emotions, Engagement, Relationships, Meaning, and Achievement.

quizQuestions.question: The hedonic adaptation principle explains why:
options: People quickly return to a baseline level of happiness after positive events
Negative events always lower well-being permanently
quizQuestions.answer: People quickly return to a baseline level of happiness after positive events
quizQuestions.explanation: Hedonic adaptation (the hedonic treadmill) shows people adapt to changes and return to a stable happiness set point.

quizQuestions.question: Flow, as defined by Csikszentmihalyi, is a state of:
options: Complete absorption in a challenging activity
quizQuestions.answer: Complete absorption in a challenging activity
quizQuestions.explanation: Flow is the optimal experience of deep, effortless concentration when skill level matches task challenge.

quizQuestions.question: Research on gratitude interventions has shown they most consistently improve:
quizQuestions.answer: Subjective well-being and positive affect
quizQuestions.explanation: Gratitude interventions reliably increase positive emotions, life satisfaction, and overall subjective well-being.

quizQuestions.question: Character strengths in positive psychology are best defined as:
options: Positive traits that contribute to individual and collective flourishing
quizQuestions.answer: Positive traits that contribute to individual and collective flourishing
quizQuestions.explanation: Peterson and Seligman's VIA classification identifies 24 character strengths as core positive traits that enable well-being.

quizQuestions.question: Industrial-Organizational (I-O) psychology applies psychological principles to:
options: Workplace behavior, performance, and organizational design
quizQuestions.answer: Workplace behavior, performance, and organizational design
quizQuestions.explanation: I-O psychology improves worker productivity, job satisfaction, and organizational effectiveness.

quizQuestions.question: A forensic psychologist is most likely to:
options: Treat patients in a hospital setting
Provide psychological expertise within the legal system
quizQuestions.answer: Provide psychological expertise within the legal system
quizQuestions.explanation: Forensic psychologists apply psychological science to legal issues such as competency evaluations and criminal profiling.

quizQuestions.question: To become a licensed clinical psychologist in the US, the minimum degree required is typically a:
quizQuestions.answer: Doctoral degree (Ph.D. or Psy.D.)
quizQuestions.explanation: Licensure as a clinical psychologist in the US requires a doctoral degree plus supervised clinical hours and a licensing exam.

quizQuestions.question: Health psychology focuses on:
options: The relationship between psychological factors and physical health
quizQuestions.answer: The relationship between psychological factors and physical health
quizQuestions.explanation: Health psychology examines how behavior, cognition, and emotion influence physical health and illness prevention.

quizQuestions.question: The American Psychological Association (APA) is best described as:
options: The principal professional organization for psychologists in the United States
quizQuestions.answer: The principal professional organization for psychologists in the United States
quizQuestions.explanation: The APA is the largest professional association for psychologists in the US, setting ethical standards and publication guidelines.

quizQuestions.question: Internal validity in an experiment refers to:
options: How well results generalize to the real world
Whether the study measures what it intends to measure
The degree to which changes in the IV caused changes in the DV
quizQuestions.answer: The degree to which changes in the IV caused changes in the DV
quizQuestions.explanation: Internal validity ensures that observed effects are due to the independent variable, not confounding factors.

quizQuestions.question: A double-blind study controls for:
quizQuestions.answer: Demand characteristics and experimenter bias
quizQuestions.explanation: Double-blind designs prevent both participants and experimenters from knowing group assignments, controlling their biases.

quizQuestions.question: Random assignment in an experiment serves the purpose of:
options: Ensuring a representative sample from the population
Equating groups on known and unknown confounding variables
quizQuestions.answer: Equating groups on known and unknown confounding variables
quizQuestions.explanation: Random assignment distributes participant characteristics equally across conditions, controlling for confounds.

quizQuestions.question: Institutional Review Boards (IRBs) exist to:
options: Evaluate the scientific merit of studies
Protect human participants from harm and rights violations
quizQuestions.answer: Protect human participants from harm and rights violations
quizQuestions.explanation: IRBs review research proposals to ensure ethical treatment of participants including informed consent and minimal risk.

quizQuestions.question: Longitudinal research designs are most useful for studying:
options: Differences between cultural groups at one time point
quizQuestions.answer: Changes within individuals over time
quizQuestions.explanation: Longitudinal designs follow the same participants over extended periods, revealing developmental and change patterns.

quizQuestions.question: A peer-reviewed journal article has been:
options: Evaluated by independent experts in the field before publication
quizQuestions.answer: Evaluated by independent experts in the field before publication
quizQuestions.explanation: Peer review is the process where independent experts evaluate a study's methodology and conclusions before publication.

quizQuestions.question: Which database is specifically designed for searching psychology and behavioral science literature?
quizQuestions.answer: PsycINFO
quizQuestions.explanation: PsycINFO is the APA's comprehensive database of psychology and behavioral science literature.

quizQuestions.question: A literature review in a research paper serves to:
options: Summarize and synthesize existing research relevant to the study topic
quizQuestions.answer: Summarize and synthesize existing research relevant to the study topic
quizQuestions.explanation: A literature review contextualizes a study within existing knowledge and identifies the research gap being addressed.

quizQuestions.question: Effect size in a research study indicates:
options: Whether the result is statistically significant
The practical magnitude of the relationship or difference
The probability the result is due to chance
quizQuestions.answer: The practical magnitude of the relationship or difference
quizQuestions.explanation: Effect size (e.g., Cohen's d) quantifies the practical significance of a finding, independent of sample size.

quizQuestions.question: A systematic review differs from a narrative review in that it:
options: Uses predetermined criteria to search and evaluate all relevant studies
Is written by a single author
quizQuestions.answer: Uses predetermined criteria to search and evaluate all relevant studies
quizQuestions.explanation: Systematic reviews follow explicit, reproducible protocols to identify and evaluate all eligible studies on a question.

quizQuestions.question: In APA 7th edition, the running head is:
options: Required only in manuscripts submitted for publication
Required only on the title page
quizQuestions.answer: Required only in manuscripts submitted for publication
quizQuestions.explanation: APA 7th edition eliminated the running head requirement for student papers; it is only required for publication submissions.

quizQuestions.question: An APA 7th edition in-text citation for a work with two authors uses:
options: The word 'and' in narrative citations, & in parenthetical citations
The ampersand (&) only in parenthetical citations inside parentheses
quizQuestions.answer: The word 'and' in narrative citations, & in parenthetical citations
quizQuestions.explanation: APA 7 uses 'and' in narrative citations (Smith and Jones, 2021) and '&' inside parentheses (Smith & Jones, 2021).

quizQuestions.question: For a journal article with three or more authors, APA 7th edition in-text citations use:
options: All author last names each time
First author et al. from the first citation
First two authors then et al.
All authors in the reference list only
quizQuestions.answer: First author et al. from the first citation
quizQuestions.explanation: APA 7th edition uses 'First Author et al.' from the very first citation when a work has three or more authors.

quizQuestions.question: An abstract in APA format should be:
options: A bulleted list of key findings
quizQuestions.answer: 150–250 words on a separate page
quizQuestions.explanation: APA abstracts are concise summaries of 150–250 words placed on a separate page after the title page.

quizQuestions.question: The reference list in APA format is organized:
options: Alphabetically by the first author's last name
quizQuestions.answer: Alphabetically by the first author's last name
quizQuestions.explanation: APA reference lists are arranged alphabetically by the first author's last name and then by year for same-author entries.

quizQuestions.question: A Likert scale typically asks participants to rate their agreement on a scale from:
quizQuestions.answer: 1 (strongly disagree) to 5 or 7 (strongly agree)
quizQuestions.explanation: Likert scales measure attitudes/opinions on an ordered response format, typically 5 or 7 points.

quizQuestions.question: Test-retest reliability refers to:
options: Whether two different tests measure the same construct
The consistency of a measure across time
Whether a test is free from cultural bias
The accuracy of a test compared to a gold standard
quizQuestions.answer: The consistency of a measure across time
quizQuestions.explanation: Test-retest reliability is established by administering the same test twice and correlating the scores.

quizQuestions.question: Social desirability bias in self-report surveys occurs when respondents:
options: Answer to present themselves favorably rather than truthfully
quizQuestions.answer: Answer to present themselves favorably rather than truthfully
quizQuestions.explanation: Social desirability bias inflates positive self-reports because people want to appear favorable to researchers.

quizQuestions.question: Operational definition in research refers to:
options: The theoretical meaning of a construct
The specific procedure used to measure or manipulate a variable
The statistical test selected for analysis
quizQuestions.answer: The specific procedure used to measure or manipulate a variable
quizQuestions.explanation: Operational definitions specify exactly how abstract variables are measured or manipulated in a study.

quizQuestions.question: Construct validity refers to whether a test:
options: Actually measures the theoretical construct it claims to measure
quizQuestions.answer: Actually measures the theoretical construct it claims to measure
quizQuestions.explanation: Construct validity is the degree to which a measurement tool accurately captures the theoretical concept it is designed to assess.

quizQuestions.question: A statistically significant result (p < .05) means:
options: The effect is large and important
There is less than a 5% probability the result is due to chance
quizQuestions.answer: There is less than a 5% probability the result is due to chance
quizQuestions.explanation: p < .05 indicates a less than 5% probability that the observed result would occur if the null hypothesis were true.

quizQuestions.question: Cohen's d is used to measure:
options: Effect size for differences between means
quizQuestions.answer: Effect size for differences between means
quizQuestions.explanation: Cohen's d expresses the standardized difference between two means, indicating practical significance.

quizQuestions.question: A positive correlation between two variables means:
options: One variable causes the other to increase
As one variable increases, the other tends to increase as well
As one increases, the other decreases
quizQuestions.answer: As one variable increases, the other tends to increase as well
quizQuestions.explanation: A positive correlation indicates a direct relationship — both variables move in the same direction.

quizQuestions.question: A confidence interval of 95% means we are 95% confident that:
options: The true population parameter falls within the interval
95% of scores fall within the interval
The study will replicate 95% of the time
quizQuestions.answer: The true population parameter falls within the interval
quizQuestions.explanation: A 95% CI means that if the study were repeated many times, 95% of intervals would contain the true population value.

quizQuestions.question: Standard deviation measures:
options: The most common score in a dataset
The middle value in a dataset
The spread or variability of scores around the mean
The difference between highest and lowest scores
quizQuestions.answer: The spread or variability of scores around the mean
quizQuestions.explanation: Standard deviation quantifies how much individual scores deviate from the mean, indicating data variability.

quizQuestions.question: The case study of Phineas Gage contributed to understanding of:
options: The role of the frontal lobe in personality and behavior
quizQuestions.answer: The role of the frontal lobe in personality and behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Gage's personality change after frontal lobe damage demonstrated the role of the frontal lobe in behavior regulation.

quizQuestions.question: Patient H.M., studied after brain surgery, demonstrated that the hippocampus is critical for:
quizQuestions.answer: Forming new explicit long-term memories
quizQuestions.explanation: H.M.'s inability to form new declarative memories after hippocampal removal established the hippocampus's role in memory.

quizQuestions.question: A key limitation of the case study method is:
options: Results from a single individual may not generalize to others
quizQuestions.answer: Results from a single individual may not generalize to others
quizQuestions.explanation: Case studies provide rich detail about individuals but have limited generalizability to broader populations.

quizQuestions.question: The case of Genie (the feral child) provided evidence for:
options: The role of rewards in language learning
A critical period for language acquisition
The importance of IQ in learning
quizQuestions.answer: A critical period for language acquisition
quizQuestions.explanation: Genie's failure to acquire full language after her discovery at age 13 supported the critical period hypothesis for language.

quizQuestions.question: Triangulation in qualitative research means:
options: Using multiple data sources or methods to strengthen conclusions
quizQuestions.answer: Using multiple data sources or methods to strengthen conclusions
quizQuestions.explanation: Triangulation improves validity by corroborating findings across multiple methods, data sources, or researchers.

quizQuestions.question: An APA research poster typically includes:
options: Introduction, methods, results, and conclusions in visual panels
quizQuestions.answer: Introduction, methods, results, and conclusions in visual panels
quizQuestions.explanation: Research posters present a study's key sections visually — introduction, methods, results, and discussion — for conference display.

quizQuestions.question: The 'so what?' question in a research presentation refers to:
options: The practical and theoretical implications of the findings
quizQuestions.answer: The practical and theoretical implications of the findings
quizQuestions.explanation: Researchers must articulate why their findings matter — the real-world or scientific significance of their results.

quizQuestions.question: When presenting research findings, limitations should be:
quizQuestions.answer: Acknowledged honestly and contextualized
quizQuestions.explanation: Ethical and credible research presentation includes transparently acknowledging methodological limitations.

quizQuestions.question: Visual aids in a research presentation are most effective when they:
options: Contain all the text from the paper
Simplify complex data into clear, labeled graphics
quizQuestions.answer: Simplify complex data into clear, labeled graphics
quizQuestions.explanation: Effective visual aids distill complex information into clear visuals that complement and enhance verbal explanation.

quizQuestions.question: Which section of a research paper explains what the findings mean and how they relate to prior research?
quizQuestions.answer: Discussion
quizQuestions.explanation: The discussion interprets findings, connects them to prior literature, notes limitations, and suggests future research.

quizQuestions.question: A capstone project in psychology is designed primarily to:
options: Demonstrate independent research and synthesis of psychological knowledge
quizQuestions.answer: Demonstrate independent research and synthesis of psychological knowledge
quizQuestions.explanation: Capstone projects integrate knowledge across a program through original research or applied projects.

quizQuestions.question: Reflective practice in psychology education involves:
options: Critically examining one's own learning, assumptions, and development
quizQuestions.answer: Critically examining one's own learning, assumptions, and development
quizQuestions.explanation: Reflective practice requires metacognitive examination of one's own thinking processes, growth, and areas for improvement.

quizQuestions.question: Future directions in a research paper should:
options: Propose specific questions or studies that logically follow from the findings
quizQuestions.answer: Propose specific questions or studies that logically follow from the findings
quizQuestions.explanation: Future directions extend the research by identifying new questions and suggesting specific studies to build on current findings.

quizQuestions.question: Academic integrity in research requires:
options: Citing only sources the professor has read
Honest representation of data and proper attribution of all ideas
Changing data that contradicts your hypothesis
Submitting the same paper for multiple classes without disclosure
quizQuestions.answer: Honest representation of data and proper attribution of all ideas
quizQuestions.explanation: Academic integrity demands honest data reporting, complete source attribution, and avoidance of fabrication, falsification, or plagiarism.

quizQuestions.question: A growth mindset, as defined by Dweck, is the belief that:
options: Intelligence and abilities are fixed at birth
Effort and learning can develop intelligence and abilities over time
Failure should be avoided at all costs
quizQuestions.answer: Effort and learning can develop intelligence and abilities over time
quizQuestions.explanation: Dweck's growth mindset is the belief that abilities are malleable and can be developed through dedication and effort.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of a falsifiable psychological hypothesis?
options: Students who sleep 8 hours will score higher on memory tests than those who sleep 4 hours
questions.answer: Students who sleep 8 hours will score higher on memory tests than those who sleep 4 hours
questions.explanation: A falsifiable hypothesis makes a specific, testable prediction that could be proven wrong by empirical data.

questions.question: The replication crisis in psychology has led researchers to prioritize:
options: Smaller sample sizes to reduce cost
questions.answer: Open science practices and pre-registration
questions.explanation: In response to replication failures, the field has adopted open science norms including pre-registration and data transparency.

questions.question: Long-term potentiation is considered a cellular mechanism underlying:
questions.answer: Synaptic learning and memory formation
questions.explanation: LTP strengthens synaptic connections with repeated activation, providing a cellular basis for learning and memory.

questions.question: Collectivist cultures, compared to individualist cultures, tend to show:
questions.answer: Greater conformity and in-group cooperation
questions.explanation: Research consistently finds greater conformity, harmony-seeking, and in-group cooperation in collectivist cultural contexts.

questions.question: Seligman's concept of 'learned helplessness' was later transformed into research on:
questions.answer: Positive psychology and resilience
questions.explanation: Seligman moved from studying learned helplessness to founding positive psychology, focusing on strengths and flourishing.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a career in applied psychology?
questions.answer: Astrophysicist
questions.explanation: Astrophysics is a physical science unrelated to psychology; forensic, school, and I-O are all applied psychology careers.

questions.question: External validity in research refers to:
options: The ability to generalize findings to other populations and settings
The accuracy of the measuring instrument
questions.answer: The ability to generalize findings to other populations and settings
questions.explanation: External validity concerns how well study results apply beyond the specific study conditions to the real world.

questions.question: A literature review should ideally be organized:
options: Thematically or by argument, synthesizing studies rather than just summarizing them
questions.answer: Thematically or by argument, synthesizing studies rather than just summarizing them
questions.explanation: Effective literature reviews organize sources thematically to build an argument, not just list individual study summaries.

questions.question: APA 7th edition requires that student papers include:
options: A running head on every page
Author affiliation, course number, and instructor name
A separate appendix for all citations
questions.answer: Author affiliation, course number, and instructor name
questions.explanation: APA 7th edition student paper title pages require student name, affiliation, course, instructor, and date.

questions.question: Social desirability bias is best minimized by:
options: Anonymous responding and behaviorally anchored questions
questions.answer: Anonymous responding and behaviorally anchored questions
questions.explanation: Anonymity reduces self-presentation concerns; behaviorally anchored questions ask about specific actions rather than attitudes.

questions.question: A p-value of .03 means:
options: There is a 3% chance the hypothesis is true
There is a 3% chance the result occurred by chance if the null is true
The study replicated 97% of previous findings
questions.answer: There is a 3% chance the result occurred by chance if the null is true
questions.explanation: The p-value is the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true.

questions.question: Patient H.M.'s case study was significant primarily because it demonstrated:
options: Language is housed in the left hemisphere
The hippocampus is essential for new explicit memory formation
Sleep is required for memory consolidation
questions.answer: The hippocampus is essential for new explicit memory formation
questions.explanation: H.M.'s anterograde amnesia following hippocampal removal proved the hippocampus is necessary for forming new declarative memories.

questions.question: When presenting research, acknowledging limitations is important because:
options: It makes the study appear weaker
It demonstrates scientific integrity and helps others evaluate the findings accurately
Journals require at least five limitations listed
It reduces the need for a discussion section
questions.answer: It demonstrates scientific integrity and helps others evaluate the findings accurately
questions.explanation: Transparent reporting of limitations is a hallmark of credible science and allows proper interpretation of results.

questions.question: Embodied cognition challenges traditional cognitive science by arguing that:
options: The brain is the sole site of cognition
Physical body experience is irrelevant to thought
Cognitive processes are grounded in bodily states and actions
questions.answer: Cognitive processes are grounded in bodily states and actions
questions.explanation: Embodied cognition holds that thought arises from interactions between brain, body, and environment — not just abstract symbols.

questions.question: The Belmont Report established which three core principles for ethical research with human participants?
options: Respect for persons, beneficence, and justice
questions.answer: Respect for persons, beneficence, and justice
questions.explanation: The Belmont Report (1979) established three ethical principles: respect for persons (autonomy), beneficence (do good/no harm), and justice (fair participant selection).

questions.question: Which approach to psychology emphasizes testing hypotheses with controlled, observable, and measurable data?
questions.answer: Scientific empiricism
questions.explanation: Scientific empiricism is the foundation of modern psychology — knowledge comes from systematic, observable, testable evidence.

questions.question: Neurogenesis in adults primarily occurs in which brain structure?
questions.answer: Hippocampus
questions.explanation: Adult neurogenesis (new neuron production) is most documented in the hippocampal dentate gyrus, related to memory and stress.

questions.question: The WEIRD problem in psychology research refers to the risk of:
options: Generalizing findings from narrow Western samples to all humanity
questions.answer: Generalizing findings from narrow Western samples to all humanity
questions.explanation: WEIRD critiques over-reliance on Western, educated, industrialized, rich, democratic samples as the basis for universal claims.

questions.question: Broaden-and-build theory (Fredrickson) proposes that positive emotions:
options: Broaden awareness and build lasting personal resources
Are less adaptive than negative emotions
questions.answer: Broaden awareness and build lasting personal resources
questions.explanation: Fredrickson's broaden-and-build theory holds that positive emotions expand cognitive repertoire and build durable physical, social, and intellectual resources.

questions.question: I-O psychology's focus on 'personnel psychology' primarily involves:
options: Hiring, training, performance appraisal, and workforce selection
questions.answer: Hiring, training, performance appraisal, and workforce selection
questions.explanation: Personnel psychology, a branch of I-O, applies psychological principles to selection, training, and performance management.

questions.question: A confounding variable in an experiment is:
options: An uncontrolled variable that varies with the independent variable
A participant characteristic selected for study
questions.answer: An uncontrolled variable that varies with the independent variable
questions.explanation: Confounds vary systematically with the IV and can produce alternative explanations for the observed effect.

questions.question: Which search term strategy is most effective when using PsycINFO?
options: Using Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT) with specific terms
Limiting searches to the past 50 years only
questions.answer: Using Boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT) with specific terms
questions.explanation: Boolean operators combine search terms precisely, narrowing or expanding results to find the most relevant literature.

questions.question: In APA 7, a DOI is best described as:
options: A permanent digital identifier linking to a specific article
An abbreviation for the journal impact factor
questions.answer: A permanent digital identifier linking to a specific article
questions.explanation: A DOI (Digital Object Identifier) is a permanent alphanumeric string assigned to a document to provide a stable hyperlink.

questions.question: Inter-rater reliability assesses:
options: Consistency of a measure across time
Agreement between two or more independent raters
Whether a test measures what it intends to
questions.answer: Agreement between two or more independent raters
questions.explanation: Inter-rater reliability measures the degree to which two or more observers independently give the same ratings or codings.

questions.question: A correlation coefficient of r = –0.75 indicates:
questions.answer: A strong negative relationship
questions.explanation: r = –0.75 indicates a strong negative correlation — as one variable increases, the other strongly decreases.

questions.question: Phineas Gage's accident demonstrated the importance of the frontal lobe for:
questions.answer: Personality and social behavior regulation
questions.explanation: After frontal lobe damage Gage showed dramatic personality changes including impulsivity and poor social judgment.

questions.question: Triangulation in qualitative research strengthens validity by:
options: Corroborating findings across multiple methods or data sources
questions.answer: Corroborating findings across multiple methods or data sources
questions.explanation: Triangulation uses multiple methods, sources, or researchers to verify and strengthen qualitative conclusions.

questions.question: The discussion section of a research paper should NOT:
questions.answer: Present raw data tables
questions.explanation: Raw data and statistical results are presented in the results section; the discussion interprets and contextualizes them.

questions.question: Academic self-efficacy, as studied in educational psychology, predicts:
options: Effort, persistence, and achievement in academic tasks
questions.answer: Effort, persistence, and achievement in academic tasks
questions.explanation: Students with high academic self-efficacy invest more effort, persist longer, and achieve more, regardless of actual ability level.

questions.question: Dweck's research on growth vs. fixed mindset has implications for education by suggesting:
options: Praising intelligence leads to greater motivation
Praising effort and process leads to greater resilience and learning
questions.answer: Praising effort and process leads to greater resilience and learning
questions.explanation: Dweck found that process praise fosters growth mindset, leading students to embrace challenges and persist through setbacks.

questions.question: Which of the following best demonstrates ecological validity?
options: A study conducted in a natural, real-world setting
A study with a very large sample size
questions.answer: A study conducted in a natural, real-world setting
questions.explanation: Ecological validity is the degree to which research findings reflect real-world conditions and behavior.

questions.question: Pre-registration of a study means researchers publicly commit to:
options: Their hypotheses and analysis plan before collecting data
questions.answer: Their hypotheses and analysis plan before collecting data
questions.explanation: Pre-registration timestamps hypotheses and planned analyses before data collection, preventing HARKing (hypothesizing after results are known).

questions.question: Which positive psychology concept refers to finding deep purpose and direction in one's activities?
questions.answer: Eudaimonia
questions.explanation: Eudaimonia (Aristotle, used in positive psychology) refers to well-being derived from living meaningfully and fulfilling one's potential.

questions.question: A capstone project demonstrates mastery primarily through:
options: Independent synthesis and application of knowledge to a novel question
questions.answer: Independent synthesis and application of knowledge to a novel question
questions.explanation: Capstone projects require students to integrate learning from a program and apply it independently to a new, substantive question.

questions.question: Research ethics requires informed consent to include:
options: The purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, and right to withdraw
questions.answer: The purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, and right to withdraw
questions.explanation: Informed consent requires disclosing study purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, confidentiality measures, and the right to withdraw without penalty.
